title: ECG — Performing
oneLiner: Standardised recording of cardiac electrical activity from surface electrodes; essential for diagnosing arrhythmias, ischaemia and structural disease, but only as good as the technique that produces it.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette (ward):** A 62‑year‑old man with central chest pain, diaphoresis and nausea is wheeled into the resuscitation bay. The nurse hands you the ECG machine. He’s lying flat, anxious, breathing fast. You clean the skin, attach electrodes in the correct positions, and record a 12‑lead. The tracing shows clear ST‑elevation in leads II, III and aVF. You recognise an inferior STEMI, activate the cath lab, and the patient is on the table within 30 minutes. **The ECG was diagnostic because the recording was technically perfect.**

- **Classic vignette (clinic/GP):** A 55‑year‑old woman with palpitations and dizziness, otherwise well. You perform a resting ECG. Leads V1 and V2 show a poor R‑wave progression and a notched R wave in V1 mimicking anterior infarction. But you recall that V1 and V2 were placed in the 2nd intercostal space (too high) because the patient had a prominent clavicle. **You repeat the ECG with correct placement – normal R‑wave progression. You saved her from an unnecessary echo and anxiety.**

- **Dissect the vignette – pattern clues:**
    - **Chest pain + diaphoresis** → high pre‑test probability for acute coronary syndrome; speed and quality of ECG acquisition are critical.
    - **Patient anxious, breathing fast** → risk of muscle tremor artefact; need for limb relaxation and minimal movement.
    - **ECG with poor R‑wave progression + atypical V1/V2 appearance** → **clue: misplacement of precordial leads** – most common error.
    - **Absence of reciprocal changes, normal QRS axis, no pathological Q waves** → supports artefact rather than true ischaemia.
    - **Repeat ECG with corrected placement → normal trace** → confirms technical error.

- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
    1. **Obesity or large breasts:** Pre‑cordial leads placed too laterally or under breast tissue. Predicted result – reduced voltage, poor R‑wave progression, mimic of chronic lung disease or anteroseptal infarct. **Clue:** note body habitus; use appropriate electrode positions (under breast, not on it) and consider placing V3/V4 at the correct intercostal space even if it requires adjustment.
    2. **Left‑arm amputation / missing limb:** You struggle with standard limb lead positions. A typical error: placing the left‑arm electrode on the left shoulder or left upper trunk. This rotates the electrical axis and produces a false left‑axis deviation. **Clue:** check limb lead anatomy – if limb is absent, place electrodes distally on the residual limb, not on the trunk. The limb leads should record from the limb, not the chest.
    3. **Severe tremor (Parkinson’s, anxiety, rigors):** Baseline wander, gross artefact that obscures P waves and ST‑segments. You may be tempted to accept the trace. **Clue:** look for regular 4‑10 Hz oscillations; ask the patient to place hands under the buttocks, relax, and breathe gently. Place the limb electrodes as low as possible (wrists/ankles) to reduce muscle interference.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain‑language definition:** The ECG is a graphic representation of the electrical depolarisation and repolarisation of the heart, recorded from electrodes placed on the skin at standardised positions. Performing it correctly means obtaining a clean, artefact‑free trace with proper lead placement so that the electrical signals are not distorted.

- **Why this topic matters clinically:**
    - **Frequency:** You will perform or supervise thousands of ECGs in your career – it is the most commonly requested bedside cardiac test.
    - **Stakes:** A poorly performed ECG can **mimic a myocardial infarction** (e.g., poor R‑wave progression from misplaced leads), **miss a dangerous arrhythmia** (e.g., fine atrial fibrillation obscured by tremor), or **lead to unnecessary interventions** (e.g., thrombolysis for a “pseudo‑STEMI” caused by lead reversal).
    - **Common pitfalls:** The average junior doctor has never been formally taught electrode placement. Errors occur in up to 40% of ward ECGs. This is a low‑effort, high‑yield skill to master.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
- **How the electrical signal reaches the surface:** The heart’s electrical activity generates a small voltage (~1‑2 mV) that propagates through the thorax. Electrodes detect this voltage relative to a reference (Wilson central terminal). The amplitude and direction depend on:
    - **Distance of the electrode from the heart** – closer = larger signal.
    - **Orientation of the electrode relative to the heart’s electrical axis** – leads facing the direction of depolarisation show positive deflections.
    - **Conductivity of the intervening tissues** – fat, air (COPD), fluid (pleural effusion) reduce amplitude.

- **Why electrode placement matters mechanistically:**
    - **Limb leads (I, II, III):** These form Einthoven’s triangle. If you place the right arm electrode on the trunk instead of the wrist, the vector for lead I rotates, producing an artefactual right‑axis deviation or P‑wave inversion that can mimic dextrocardia or limb‑lead reversal.
    - **Precordial leads (V1–V6):** They are positioned at specific intercostal spaces relative to the heart’s anatomical landmarks. Moving V1 or V2 up by one interspace (e.g., to the 2nd intercostal space) moves the electrode away from the right ventricle, causing loss of the normal R wave and a QS pattern – this is the most common mimic of an **anteroseptal infarct**.
    - **The “poor R‑wave progression” trap:** Normal R‑wave progression (V1: small R, larger S → V6: large R, small S) depends on the chest leads being placed in the correct horizontal plane. If all V leads are placed too high (e.g., along the 2nd/3rd intercostal space), the R wave may remain small in V5/V6, creating a pattern of “poor R‑wave progression” that is mistakenly attributed to anterior myocardial infarction.

- **Connection to pattern recognition cues:**
    - In the vignette with misplaced V1/V2, the loss of R wave and notched R in V1 arose because the electrodes were placed above the usual level of the right ventricular outflow tract. The normal septal Q wave was exaggerated, and the anterior forces were not recorded.
    - In the tremor vignette, baseline oscillations are due to skeletal muscle action potentials (voltage ~50 µV to 1 mV) that interfere with the low‑amplitude P wave. Filters can reduce 60‑Hz noise but not muscle tremor – you must ask the patient to relax.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers of Poor ECG Quality:
This section covers causes of sub‑optimal recording – the “why” your ECG may be uninterpretable.

| Category | Common Cause | 1‑Line Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|------------------|
| **Operator error** | Misplaced pre‑cordial leads (most common) | “ECG shows poor R‑wave progression but the patient is young and otherwise healthy.” |
| | Limb lead reversal (right arm/left arm) | “Lead I shows a negative P wave, inverted QRS, and aVR is upright.” |
| | Electrodes placed on trunk instead of limbs | “P wave appears smaller, limb lead voltages are low.” |
| | Wrong calibration (e.g., 20 mm/mV) | “Tall, broad QRS complexes – check calibration mark (should be 10 mm/mV).” |
| **Patient factors** | Tremor (Parkinson’s, anxiety, rigors) | “Baseline wander, no clear P waves, artefact at 4‑10 Hz.” |
| | Restlessness / movement | “Repeated abrupt baseline shifts, especially during recording.” |
| | Excessive hair / sweat / oily skin | “Electrode contact poor – note high‑frequency noise.” |
| | Obesity | “Low voltage and poor R‑wave progression.” |
| | Chest wall deformity (pectus excavatum, scoliosis) | “QRS axis shifts; consider reporting lead positions.” |
| **Equipment issues** | Broken lead wires or expired electrodes | “Intermittent loss of one lead, or no signal in a single lead.” |
| | Electrical interference (60‑Hz mains noise) | “Fine, regular 50/60‑Hz oscillations – common in old buildings or near monitors.” |
| **Drug effects** (prevent adequate recording) | None directly; but drugs can cause tremor (e.g., lithium, beta‑agonists) or electrolyte changes that affect signal | “ECG with high T waves and wide QRS – but technical quality poor; repeat after smoothing.” |

**Most common cause:** misplacement of pre‑cordial leads, especially V1 and V2 being recorded in the 2nd intercostal space instead of the 4th.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
This section treats the **ECG itself as the investigation being performed**. The “what” is the technical quality criteria.

**Diagnostic criteria for a “good” ECG:**
- **Calibration:** 10 mm = 1 mV (check the rectangular calibration pulse at the beginning of the tracing).
- **Paper speed:** 25 mm/s (each small box = 0.04 s, large box = 0.20 s).
- **All 12 leads visible** with <3 leads showing significant artefact.
- **Limb leads:** P wave upright in II, inverted in aVR (normal axis).
- **Precordial leads:** Normal R‑wave progression (R wave becomes taller than S wave by V4).
- **Baseline:** Flat, no wander >1 mm.

| Test / Check | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|--------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Pre‑cordial lead placement check** | Which intercostal spaces were used. | To confirm correct position. | Use palpable landmarks: Angle of Louis (2nd rib) → count down to 4th intercostal space. V1 and V2 at 4th ICS. V4 at 5th ICS mid‑clavicular. V5/V6 at same horizontal level. |
| **Limb lead position check** | Whether electrodes are on limbs or trunk. | To avoid axis artefact. | Arms: midway between wrist and elbow, not on shoulder. Legs: lateral malleoli or calves. If limb amputated, place on distal residual limb. |
| **Calibration check** | Amplitude scaling. | To confirm voltages are real. | The calibration pulse height should be 10 mm (two large squares). If only 5 mm, signals are over‑amplified; if 20 mm, under‑amplified. |
| **Filter settings** | Muscle tremor vs. pacemaker spikes. | Frequently set to 0.05–150 Hz – if “monitor” filter (0.5–40 Hz) used, you may miss subtle ST changes or pacemaker spikes. | Prefer diagnostic mode. If artefact persists, use monitor filter only as a last resort and document “filtered recording”. |

**Pattern‑recognition: what single finding confirms a technically sound ECG?**
- **A clean, stable baseline** with all 12 leads clearly visible and the calibration mark exactly 10 mm tall.

**What single finding rules out a valid ECG?**
- **Lead I showing a completely inverted P‑wave and QRS** – this is limb‑lead reversal until proven otherwise. Repeat.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
#### Immediate (first hour) – on the ward or in the ED | Action | What + How | Why | |--------|------------|-----| | **Patient preparation** | Explain procedure, ensure supine position (head of bed ≤30°), arms by sides, legs uncrossed. | Reduces muscle tension, prevents baseline shift from leg movement. | | **Skin preparation** | Clean skin with alcohol wipe, clip hair if necessary, dry thoroughly. | Good contact = low impedance = less noise. | | **Electrode placement** | **Limb leads:** White (right arm) – midway between wrist & elbow, on radial side. Black (left arm) – same location on left. Red (left leg) – lateral malleolus or calf. Green (right leg) – lateral malleolus, ground.
**Precordial leads:** V1 – 4th intercostal space, right sternal border. V2 – 4th ICS, left sternal border. V3 – midway between V2 and V4. V4 – 5th ICS, mid‑clavicular line. V5 – anterior axillary line, same horizontal level as V4. V6 – mid‑axillary line, same level. | Standardisation ensures reproducibility and accurate axis/pathology interpretation. | | **Recording** | Press “Auto” or record for 10 seconds (standard). Ensure no movement during recording. | Captures at least 3 complexes per lead for rate and rhythm analysis. | | **Quick quality check** | Look at lead II: P wave upright? aVR: P wave inverted? Baseline flat? Calibration mark 10 mm? | Immediate detection of lead reversal or artefact. If abnormal, repeat immediately. | #### Ward (24–72h) – repetitive recordings or telemetry - **For serial ECGs (e.g., chest pain observation):** Mark the position of V1/V2 on the skin with a permanent marker or note the measured distance from the angle of Louis. This ensures identical placement for comparison. - **For patients with tremor:** Use the “limb lead modification” – place electrodes on the shoulders and lower abdomen only if you cannot tolerate limb leads elsewhere. Document this in the notes. - **For bariatric patients:** Place V3/V4 at the correct intercostal space, but if the breast tissue is large, lift the breast gently and place the electrode below it on the chest wall, not on the breast. #### Long-term – education and system improvement - **Audit:** Track how many ward ECGs show poor R‑wave progression or reversed leads. Provide feedback to nursing and medical staff. - **Training:** Demonstrate placement on a colleague every month. Use a diagram in the ward. - **What NOT to do:** - **Do not accept an ECG with gross artefact and “interpret” it anyway** – you will miss atrial fibrillation, ST changes, or pacemaker spikes. - **Do not place limb leads on the trunk** – this reduces voltage and rotates the axis; it is the cause of many “low voltage” false reports. - **Do not place chest leads under a bra strap** – the strap pressure can cause chest‑wall movement and baseline wander. ---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | **Normal ECG (correct technique)** | **Limb‑lead reversal (RA/LA)** | **Precordial leads too high** | **Tremor artefact** |
|---------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Lead I** | P wave upright, QRS upright | P wave inverted, QRS inverted (negative deflection) | Normal | Fine oscillations (4–10 Hz) |
| **aVR** | P wave inverted | P wave upright (positive) | Normal | Oscillations |
| **V1–V2** | Small R, larger S | Normal | QS pattern or very small R (loss of R wave) | Oscillations may mimic P waves |
| **R‑wave progression** | Progressive increase; R > S by V4 | Normal | Delayed; R may not become >S until V5/V6 | Normal but obscured |
| **Baseline** | Flat | Flat | Flat | Wavy, unsteady |
| **Key clue** | All leads look normal | “Lead I is inverted while aVR is upright” | Clinical context: patient thin, chest leads appear visibly high (e.g., above nipple line) | No clinical correlation; request patient to relax and repeat |
| **Action** | None | Repeat with correct arm leads | Reposition V1/V2 to 4th ICS | Have patient place hands under buttocks, breathe calmly; repeat |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Missed correct technique → misdiagnosis:**
    - **Complication:** False diagnosis of **anteroseptal myocardial infarction** due to misplaced V1/V2 leads (too high). Consequence: unnecessary hospital admission, cardiology referral, anxiety, possibly inappropriate anti‑coagulation/thrombolysis with bleeding risk.
    - **How it presents:** The ECG shows a “QS” pattern in V1–V3 with poor R‑wave progression. The patient may have no chest pain, normal troponin, but the ECG looks “old” or “new”. The clinician must examine the leads – if they’re in the 2nd ICS, repeat.
    - **What to do:** Recognise the pattern: loss of R wave without reciprocal changes. Check lead placement by palpating the sternal angle (2nd rib) and feeling down to the 4th ICS. Reposition and repeat.

- **Complication:** Limb‑lead reversal mistaken for **dextrocardia or aVR‑dominant axis**.
    - **How it presents:** Lead I sees a negative QRS; aVR is upright. The clinician may waste time arranging an echocardiogram or chest X‑ray to rule out situs inversus.
    - **What to do:** Look at V1–V6: if R‑wave progression is normal (unlike true dextrocardia where it is reversed), then it’s lead reversal. Also check the heel of the foot – the green (RL) lead is placed on the right side for ground; it doesn’t cause reversal. Swap the RA and LA leads and repeat.

- **Missed rhythm detection due to tremor artefact:**
    - **Complication:** Atrial fibrillation (AF) with rapid ventricular rate is hidden by baseline tremor; patient remains in AF and suffers a stroke.
    - **Late‑presentation pattern:** The patient presents months later with a stroke. The old ECG was reported as “sinus rhythm with muscle tremor”. On review, the irregularly irregular rhythm was obscured.
    - **What to do:** If you see a regular‑looking rhythm on a tremulous baseline, **always use a longer rhythm strip** (e.g., 30 seconds) or ask the patient to hold still. If you are still unsure, apply pressure to the limb leads to dampen tremor, or use the “monitor filter” as a last resort (document).

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonic for limb lead placement (a common cup‑coloured system):**
    - **“White on right, snow over grass, smoke over fire.”**
        - **White** (RA) on the **right** arm.
        - **Snow** (white) over **grass** (green: RL) – i.e., white lead is on the right, green on the right (ground).
        - **Smoke** (black: LA) over **fire** (red: LL) – black on left, red on left.
    - Alternatively: **“White – Right, Black – Left, Red – Left (leg).”**

- **Mnemonic for pre‑cordial lead positioning (the numbers):**
    - **“V1 and V2 at 4, V4 at 5, same level for V5 and V6.”**
    - **“4 spaces, 4 leads (V1–V4), 4th intercostal space for V1, V2, and then V3 halfway to V4 at 5th space.”**
        - V1: 4th ICS right sternal border.
        - V2: 4th ICS left sternal border.
        - V4: 5th ICS left mid‑clavicular line.
        - V3: midway between V2 and V4.
        - V5: anterior axillary line at same horizontal level as V4.
        - V6: mid‑axillary line at same level.

**High‑Yield Pearls:**

1. **The angle of Louis is your friend.** It’s the bony ridge at the sternomanubrial junction – that’s the 2nd rib. Count down two more spaces to find the 4th intercostal space for V1 and V2. If you place V1 and V2 by guessing “nipple line” (which is often the 5th ICS in men, but lower in women), you will be too low.
2. **If a patient has had a previous ECG, look at the lead positions marked on the chest** – often V1 and V2 are at the sternal border, but if they were placed at the 2nd ICS earlier, you must correct them for serial comparison.
3. **Limb leads on the arms should be at the wrists** – not the shoulders. Shoulder placement artificially rotates the QRS axis and lowers voltage.
4. **Check the calibration mark** every time – a tall calibration mark (20 mm) means the ECG is actually half‑voltage; a short mark (5 mm) means double voltage. Misreading may lead to false LVH or low voltage.
5. **Never accept an ECG with >3 leads showing artefact** – it is a clinical waste and a medicolegal trap.
6. **In obese patients, place V1–V2 lower** (at the 5th ICS if needed) to be close to the right ventricle; otherwise you get a false poor R‑wave progression.
7. **For pacemaker patients,** ensure the pacemaker spike is visible. If you use a filter, you might filter it out. Prefer diagnostic mode; if artefact is too much, use a wider filter (0.05–100 Hz) instead of monitor mode.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- **Placing the chest electrodes in a straight line across the chest** – they must be at different horizontal levels (V1/V2 at 4th ICS, V4 at 5th ICS, V5/V6 at same level as V4).
- **Applying electrodes over clothing, tape, or on hairy skin without shaving** – leads will fall off or produce noise.
- **Forgetting to record the calibration mark** – modern machines default to 10 mm/mV, but always verify.
- **Not checking the patient’s ID or date of birth** – you may end up doing an ECG on the wrong patient.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **Call your senior (registrar/SPR) if:**
    - You are unable to obtain a readable ECG after three attempts with correct technique (e.g., patient severely tremulous, uncooperative, or agitated). The senior may decide to use a continuous telemetry monitor, a shorter recording, or sedation.
    - The ECG shows a **new arrhythmia** (e.g., atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular rate, ventricular tachycardia, atrial flutter) – this is not about performance but about recognition; escalate for management.
    -

If artefact persists, use monitor filter only as a last resort and document “filtered recording”. | **Pattern‑recognition: what single finding confirms a technically sound ECG?** - **A clean, stable baseline** with all 12 leads clearly visible and the calibration mark exactly 10 mm tall. **What single finding rules out a valid ECG?** - **Lead I showing a completely inverted P‑wave and QRS** – this is limb‑lead reversal until proven otherwise. Repeat. --- ### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning #### Immediate (first hour) – on the ward or in the ED | Action | What + How | Why | |--------|------------|-----| | **Patient preparation** | Explain procedure, ensure supine position (head of bed ≤30°), arms by sides, legs uncrossed. | Reduces muscle tension, prevents baseline shift from leg movement. | | **Skin preparation** | Clean skin with alcohol wipe, clip hair if necessary, dry thoroughly. | Good contact = low impedance = less noise. | | **Electrode placement** | **Limb leads:** White (right arm) – midway between wrist & elbow, on radial side. Black (left arm) – same location on left. Red (left leg) – lateral malleolus or calf. Green (right leg) – lateral malleolus, ground.
**Precordial leads:** V1 – 4th intercostal space, right sternal border. V2 – 4th ICS, left sternal border. V3 – midway between V2 and V4. V4 – 5th ICS, mid‑clavicular line. V5 – anterior axillary line, same horizontal level as V4. V6 – mid‑axillary line, same level. | Standardisation ensures reproducibility and accurate axis/pathology interpretation. | | **Recording** | Press “Auto” or record for 10 seconds (standard). Ensure no movement during recording. | Captures at least 3 complexes per lead for rate and rhythm analysis. | | **Quick quality check** | Look at lead II: P wave upright? aVR: P wave inverted? Baseline flat? Calibration mark 10 mm? | Immediate detection of lead reversal or artefact. If abnormal, repeat immediately. | #### Ward (24–72h) – repetitive recordings or telemetry - **For serial ECGs (e.g., chest pain observation):** Mark the position of V1/V2 on the skin with a permanent marker or note the measured distance from the angle of Louis. This ensures identical placement for comparison. - **For patients with tremor:** Use the “limb lead modification” – place electrodes on the shoulders and lower abdomen only if you cannot tolerate limb leads elsewhere. Document this in the notes. - **For bariatric patients:** Place V3/V4 at the correct intercostal space, but if the breast tissue is large, lift the breast gently and place the electrode below it on the chest wall, not on the breast. #### Long-term – education and system improvement - **Audit:** Track how many ward ECGs show poor R‑wave progression or reversed leads. Provide feedback to nursing and medical staff. - **Training:** Demonstrate placement on a colleague every month. Use a diagram in the ward. - **What NOT to do:** - **Do not accept an ECG with gross artefact and “interpret” it anyway** – you will miss atrial fibrillation, ST changes, or pacemaker spikes. - **Do not place limb leads on the trunk** – this reduces voltage and rotates the axis; it is the cause of many “low voltage” false reports. - **Do not place chest leads under a bra strap** – the strap pressure can cause chest‑wall movement and baseline wander. --- ### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table | Feature | **Normal ECG (correct technique)** | **Limb‑lead reversal (RA/LA)** | **Precordial leads too high** | **Tremor artefact** | |---------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------| | **Lead I** | P wave upright, QRS upright | P wave inverted, QRS inverted (negative deflection) | Normal | Fine oscillations (4–10 Hz) | | **aVR** | P wave inverted | P wave upright (positive) | Normal | Oscillations | | **V1–V2** | Small R, larger S | Normal | QS pattern or very small R (loss of R wave) | Oscillations may mimic P waves | | **R‑wave progression** | Progressive increase; R > S by V4 | Normal | Delayed; R may not become >S until V5/V6 | Normal but obscured | | **Baseline** | Flat | Flat | Flat | Wavy, unsteady | | **Key clue** | All leads look normal | “Lead I is inverted while aVR is upright” | Clinical context: patient thin, chest leads appear visibly high (e.g., above nipple line) | No clinical correlation; request patient to relax and repeat | | **Action** | None | Repeat with correct arm leads | Reposition V1/V2 to 4th ICS | Have patient place hands under buttocks, breathe calmly; repeat | --- ### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It? - **Missed correct technique → misdiagnosis:** - **Complication:** False diagnosis of **anteroseptal myocardial infarction** due to misplaced V1/V2 leads (too high). Consequence: unnecessary hospital admission, cardiology referral, anxiety, possibly inappropriate anti‑coagulation/thrombolysis with bleeding risk. - **How it presents:** The ECG shows a “QS” pattern in V1–V3 with poor R‑wave progression. The patient may have no chest pain, normal troponin, but the ECG looks “old” or “new”. The clinician must examine the leads – if they’re in the 2nd ICS, repeat. - **What to do:** Recognise the pattern: loss of R wave without reciprocal changes. Check lead placement by palpating the sternal angle (2nd rib) and feeling down to the 4th ICS. Reposition and repeat. - **Complication:** Limb‑lead reversal mistaken for **dextrocardia or aVR‑dominant axis**. - **How it presents:** Lead I sees a negative QRS; aVR is upright. The clinician may waste time arranging an echocardiogram or chest X‑ray to rule out situs inversus. - **What to do:** Look at V1–V6: if R‑wave progression is normal (unlike true dextrocardia where it is reversed), then it’s lead reversal. Also check the heel of the foot – the green (RL) lead is placed on the right side for ground; it doesn’t cause reversal. Swap the RA and LA leads and repeat. - **Missed rhythm detection due to tremor artefact:** - **Complication:** Atrial fibrillation (AF) with rapid ventricular rate is hidden by baseline tremor; patient remains in AF and suffers a stroke. - **Late‑presentation pattern:** The patient presents months later with a stroke. The old ECG was reported as “sinus rhythm with muscle tremor”. On review, the irregularly irregular rhythm was obscured. - **What to do:** If you see a regular‑looking rhythm on a tremulous baseline, **always use a longer rhythm strip** (e.g., 30 seconds) or ask the patient to hold still. If you are still unsure, apply pressure to the limb leads to dampen tremor, or use the “monitor filter” as a last resort (document). --- ### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls - **Mnemonic for limb lead placement (a common cup‑coloured system):** - **“White on right, snow over grass, smoke over fire.”** - **White** (RA) on the **right** arm. - **Snow** (white) over **grass** (green: RL) – i.e., white lead is on the right, green on the right (ground). - **Smoke** (black: LA) over **fire** (red: LL) – black on left, red on left. - Alternatively: **“White – Right, Black – Left, Red – Left (leg).”** - **Mnemonic for pre‑cordial lead positioning (the numbers):** - **“V1 and V2 at 4, V4 at 5, same level for V5 and V6.”** - **“4 spaces, 4 leads (V1–V4), 4th intercostal space for V1, V2, and then V3 halfway to V4 at 5th space.”** - V1: 4th ICS right sternal border. - V2: 4th ICS left sternal border. - V4: 5th ICS left mid‑clavicular line. - V3: midway between V2 and V4. - V5: anterior axillary line at same horizontal level as V4. - V6: mid‑axillary line at same level. **High‑Yield Pearls:** 1. **The angle of Louis is your friend.** It’s the bony ridge at the sternomanubrial junction – that’s the 2nd rib. Count down two more spaces to find the 4th intercostal space for V1 and V2. If you place V1 and V2 by guessing “nipple line” (which is often the 5th ICS in men, but lower in women), you will be too low. 2. **If a patient has had a previous ECG, look at the lead positions marked on the chest** – often V1 and V2 are at the sternal border, but if they were placed at the 2nd ICS earlier, you must correct them for serial comparison. 3. **Limb leads on the arms should be at the wrists** – not the shoulders. Shoulder placement artificially rotates the QRS axis and lowers voltage. 4. **Check the calibration mark** every time – a tall calibration mark (20 mm) means the ECG is actually half‑voltage; a short mark (5 mm) means double voltage. Misreading may lead to false LVH or low voltage. 5. **Never accept an ECG with >3 leads showing artefact** – it is a clinical waste and a medicolegal trap. 6. **In obese patients, place V1–V2 lower** (at the 5th ICS if needed) to be close to the right ventricle; otherwise you get a false poor R‑wave progression. 7. **For pacemaker patients,** ensure the pacemaker spike is visible. If you use a filter, you might filter it out. Prefer diagnostic mode; if artefact is too much, use a wider filter (0.05–100 Hz) instead of monitor mode. **Common mistakes to avoid:** - **Placing the chest electrodes in a straight line across the chest** – they must be at different horizontal levels (V1/V2 at 4th ICS, V4 at 5th ICS, V5/V6 at same level as V4). - **Applying electrodes over clothing, tape, or on hairy skin without shaving** – leads will fall off or produce noise. - **Forgetting to record the calibration mark** – modern machines default to 10 mm/mV, but always verify. - **Not checking the patient’s ID or date of birth** – you may end up doing an ECG on the wrong patient. --- ### 10. Escalation Criteria - **Call your senior (registrar/SPR) if:** - You are unable to obtain a readable ECG after three attempts with correct technique (e.g., patient severely tremulous, uncooperative, or agitated). The senior may decide to use a continuous telemetry monitor, a shorter recording, or sedation. - The ECG shows a **new arrhythmia** (e.g., atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular rate, ventricular tachycardia, atrial flutter) – this is not about performance but about recognition; escalate for management. -
